reference.name: Rosenkranz1996a
reference.title: Pharmacokinetics and safety of glimepiride at clinically effective doses in diabetic patients with renal impairment.
reference.abstract: The pharmacokinetics, efficacy and safety of glimepiride were investigated in a single- and a multiple-dose open study in patients with non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus and renal impairment and an initial creatinine clearance above 10 ml/ min. Patients were divided into three groups with creatinine clearance above 50 ml/min, 20-50 ml/min and under 20 ml/min. Fifteen fasting patients received a single dose of 3 mg glimepiride and serial blood and urine samples were taken over 24 h for pharmacokinetic and efficacy analyses. A further 16 patients received glimepiride over a 3-month period, an initial dose of 1 mg glimepiride being adjusted within the range 1 to 8 mg to achieve good glucose control. Pharmacokinetic evaluation was done on day 1 and after 3 months. Mean relative total clearance and mean volume of distribution of both single (41.6 ml/ min and 8.47 litres, respectively, when creatinine clearance was above 50 ml/min) and multiple doses of glimepiride increased in proportion to the degree of renal impairment (to 91.1 ml/min and 14.98 litres, respectively, when creatinine clearance was below 20 ml/min, single dose), whereas the terminal halflife and mean time remained unchanged. Lower relative total clearance and renal clearance of both glimepiride metabolites correlated significantly with lower creatinine clearance values. Of the 16 patients 12 required between 1 and 4 mg glimepiride to stabilize their fasting blood glucose. Glimepiride was well-tolerated and there were no drug-related adverse events. In conclusion glimepiride is safe, effective and has clearly-definable pharmacokinetics in diabetic patients with renal impairment. The increased plasma elimination of glimepiride with decreasing kidney function is explainable on the basis of altered protein binding with an increase in unbound drug.

descriptions.text: The pharmacokinetics, efficacy and safety of glimepiride were investigated in a single- and a multiple-dose open study in patients with non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus and renal impairment and an initial creatinine clearance above 10 ml/ min.
Patients were divided into three groupswith creatinine clearance above 50 ml/min, 20-50 ml/min and under 20 ml/min.
Fifteen fasting patients received a single dose of 3 mg glimepiride and serial blood and urine samples were taken over 24 h for pharmacokinetic and efficacy analyses

A further 16 patients received glimepiride over a 3-month period, an initial dose of 1 mg glimepiride being adjusted within the range 1 to 8 mg to achieve good glucose control. Pharmacokinetic evaluation was done on day 1 and after 3 months.

Eligible adults had NIDDM treated with insulin for the single-dose study (S), and treated for at least 4 weeks with either insulin ( ≤ 24 units daily) or oral hypoglycaemic agents for the multiple-dose study (M); they were aged between 44-75 years with a creatinine clearance above 10 ml/min

Single Dose Study: On the study day a single dose of 3 mg glimepiride was given after an overnight fast with 150 ml mineral water or decaffeinated coffee. Subjects received breakfast 30 min after administration and light meals at 2-3 h intervals throughout the day. Subjects remained recumbent for 4 h and under medical supervision for at least 48 h post-administration. Body weight and vital signs were measured and blood and urine samples for standard analyses, including frequent glucose determinations, taken immediately before and 24 h after administration of the drug. Adverse events were monitored and recorded during the study

Multiple Dose Study: The initial dose of glimepiride was 1 mg. This dose was adjusted during the study to achieve sufficient metabolic control as assessed by fasting blood glucose levels; the increasing daily doses were 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8 mg. The decision to adjust a daily dose was made 4- 7 days after the first dose of glimepiride and subsequently every 4-7 days after each dose adjustment for the total study period of 3 months. A fasting blood glucose level equal to or less than 8.32 mmol/l was acceptable in this patient population and necessitated no dose adjustment.
In both studies, patients were selected and grouped according to their baseline creatinine clearance values.

reference.name: Shukla2004
reference.title: Glimepiride pharmacokinetics in obese versus non-obese diabetic patients.
reference.abstract: Type 2 diabetes is a global concern, accounting for the vast majority of cases of diabetes. Type 2 diabetes is associated with insulin deficiency and insulin resistance and, increasingly, with patients who are overweight or obese. Glimepiride is a popular choice of oral antidiabetic agent for patients with Type 2 diabetes since it increases both insulin secretion and insulin sensitivity and, unlike some other oral agents, is associated with weight neutrality or even weight loss.

descriptions.text: To assess the pharmacokinetic characteristics of glimepiride and its metabolites in normal-weight and morbidly obese patients with type 2 diabetes to determine whether the pharmacokinetics of glimepiride are altered by obesity.

Normal-weight (n = 14) and morbidly obese (n = 14) men and women (in a 1:1 ratio) with type 2 diabetes received a single oral dose of glimepiride 8 mg following an overnight fast. Serum concentrations of glimepiride and its metabolites, cyclohexyl hydroxymethyl derivative (MI) and carboxyl derivative (MII), and urinary concentrations of these metabolites were measured.

Fourteen patients were of normal body weight and 14 were morbidly obese; there were 7 men and 7 women in each weight group. The age distribution of the 28 patients was 58.1 +- 8.8 years (range 42-69) and 49.2 +- 8.6 years (37-63) for the normalweight and obese groups, respectively. The weight distribution was 72.2 +- 10.0 kg (54-90) and 130.0 +- 14.9 kg (114-168) for the normal-weight and obese groups, respectively.

Men and nonpregnant women aged 18-70 years with type 2 diabetes were included in the study. Normal weight was defined as being within 10% of the ideal body weight range appropriate for each patient's gender, height, and frame. Patients were classified as morbidly obese if their body weight was >=200% of the minimal ideal weight for gender, height, and frame. Patients with clinically important abnormalities identified during physical examination were excluded from the study. Laboratory values (other than those relating to diabetes) and an electrocardiogram (ECG) had to be normal.

Twenty-eight patients with type 2 diabetes were enrolled in this single-center (Hazleton Clinics, West Palm Beach, FL), open-label, parallel group, Phase I study to assess the pharmacokinetic characteristics of a single dose of glimepiride 8 mg. Glimepiride was administered with water as a single dose of two 4-mg tablets after at least an 8-hour overnight fast. This high dose of glimepiride was selected to fully characterize its pharmacokinetics at a dose at the upper end of the recommended range.
Serum concentrations of glimepiride, MI, and MII, as well as urine concentrations of MI and MII, were measured by a validated HPLC assay at a wavelength of 350 nm following precolumn derivatization.

Fourteen patients were of normal body weight and 14 were morbidly obese; there were 7 men and 7 women in each weight group. The age distribution of the 28 patients was 58.1 +- 8.8 years (range 42-69) and 49.2 +- 8.6 years (37-63) for the normalweight and obese groups, respectively. The weight distribution was 72.2 +- 10.0 kg (54-90) and 130.0 +- 14.9 kg (114-168) for the normal-weight and obese groups, respectively.
Glimepiride was administered with water as a single dose of two 4-mg tablets after at least an 8-hour overnight fast

Serum concentrations of glimepiride, MI, and MII, as well as urine concentrations of MI and MII, were measured by a validated HPLC assay at a wavelength of 350 nm following precolumn derivatization.16 Only the metabolites were assayed in urine, since the kidneys do not excrete unchanged glimepiride. The assay detection limit in serum samples was 5 ng/mL for glimepiride and MII and 10 ng/mL for MI. The assay detection limit for MI and MII in urine samples was >=50 ng/mL.

reference.name: Suzuki2006
reference.title: Effect of CYP2C9 genetic polymorphisms on the efficacy and pharmacokinetics of glimepiride in subjects with type 2 diabetes.
reference.abstract: Glimepiride, a sulfonylurea hypoglycemic agent, is metabolized by cytochrome P450 2C9 (CYP2C9) which is known to have genetic polymorphisms. To examine the effects of CYP2C9 genetic polymorphisms on the safety and efficacy of glimepiride in patients with type 2 diabetes, the responses to the glimepiride were measured in Japanese type 2 diabetic patients with the different CYP2C9 genotype. The reduction in the HbA(1c) was significantly larger (P<0.05) among the CYP2C9*1/*3 subjects than among the CYP2C9*1/*1 subjects. The long-term observations of 2 patients with a CYP2C9*1/*3 suggested that subjects with a CYP2C9*1/*3 respond well to glimepiride during the initial phase of treatment, but 1 patient have shown the weight gain over the long-term treatment. The pharmacokinetic study showed that the area under the concentration-time curve for glimepiride in the CYP2C9*1/*3 subjects was approximately 2.5-fold higher than that of the CYP2C9*1/*1 subjects. The intrinsic clearance of glimepiride by the CYP2C9*3 enzyme was lower than that by the CYP2C9*1 enzyme. These results suggested that the lower hydroxylation activity of glimepiride in the subject with type 2 diabetes and CYP2C9*1/*3 led to a marked elevation in the plasma concentrations of glimepiride and a stronger pharmacological effect of glimepiride.

descriptions.text: To examine the effects of CYP2C9 genetic polymorphisms on the safety and efficacy of glimepiride in patients with type 2 diabetes, the responses to the glimepiride were measured in Japanese type 2 diabetic patients with the different CYP2C9 genotype.
Japanese patients with type 2 diabetes between the ages of 35 and 85 years who had received medical treatment with glimepiride at Nerima General Hospital were recruited for the study.
Patients with impaired liver function (aspartate aminotransferase >50 IU/l or alanine aminotransferase >50 IU/ l), and/or who had received other CYP2C9-substrate drugs were excluded.
After the CYP2C9 genotype analysis, 6 patients (4 patients with a CYP2C9*1/*1 and 2 patients with a CYP2C9*1/*3) were asked to participate in a glimepiride pharmacokinetic study.
These 6 patients ingested 1 mg of glimepiride (Amaryl, 1 mg tablet; Aventis, Japan) after eating a meal between 7 and 8 a.m.
These 6 patients ingested 1 mg of glimepiride (Amaryl, 1 mg tablet; Aventis, Japan) after eating a meal between 7 and 8 a.m.
Plasma glimepiride concentrations, blood glucose levels (BS), and immunoreactive insulin (IRI) were measured.

reference.name: Wang2005
reference.title: Pharmacokinetics of glimepiride and cytochrome P450 2C9 genetic polymorphisms.

descriptions.text: We assessed the pharmacokinetics of oral administration of glimepiride in relation to CYP2C9 genetic polymorphism in healthy Chinese subjects.
All volunteers were determined to be healthy and signed informed consent forms before participation.
CYP2C9 genotype was determined by oligonucleotide microarray as described earlier
Nineteen subjects (9 expressing CYP2C9*1/*1, 9 expressing CYP2C9*1/*3, and 1 expressing CYP2C9*3/*3) were invited to participate in the phenotype phase of the study.
Blood and urine samples were collected after 4 mg glimepiride was administered orally. Plasma glimepiride and urine M1 and M2 concentrations were determined by HPLC.
Nineteen subjects (9 expressing CYP2C9*1/*1, 9 expressing CYP2C9*1/*3, and 1 expressing CYP2C9*3/*3) were invited to participate in the phenotype phase of the study.
4 mg glimepiride was administered orally.
Blood and urine samples were collected after 4 mg glimepiride was administered orally. Plasma glimepiride and urine M1 and M2 concentrations were determined by HPLC.

reference.name: Yoo2011
reference.title: Population pharmacokinetic analysis of glimepiride with CYP2C9 genetic polymorphism in healthy Korean subjects.
reference.abstract: The purpose of this study was to develop a population pharmacokinetic (PPK) model of glimepiride and to investigate the influence of genetic polymorphisms in CYP2C9 on the PPK of glimepiride in healthy Korean subjects.

descriptions.text: A total of 177 healthy Korean male volunteers participated in this study.
The subjects' ages ranged from 19 to 30 years (mean±SD 23.1±2.2 years) and weights from 44 to 106.3 kg (mean±SD 67.6±9.5 kg).
After an overnight fast, the subjects in each study received a single oral dose of 2 mg of glimepiride with 240 mL of water (Amaryl® 2 mg tablet; Handok-Aventis, Seoul, Korea).
Blood samples were collected in Vacutainer® tubes before dosing and at 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, and 12 h after drug administration.

Of the 177 individuals analyzed for the CYP2C9*3 allele, 163 were homozygous for the CYP2C9*1 allele (*1/*1, 92.09%), and 14 were heterozygous for CYP2C9*3 allele (*1/*3, 7.91%). No subject homozygous for CYP2C9*3 was identified among the 177 Korean subjects. The allele frequencies of CYP2C9*1 and *3 were thus 96.05 and 3.95%, respectively.
After an overnight fast, the subjects in each study received a single oral dose of 2 mg of glimepiride with 240 mL of water (Amaryl® 2 mg tablet; Handok-Aventis, Seoul, Korea).

reference.name: Allonen1981
reference.title: Midazolam kinetics.
reference.abstract: The effect-kinetics of the new benzodiazepine midazolam was evaluated in six subjects after single oral (7.5 and 15 mg) and intravenous (0.075 mg/kg) doses and infusion programs. The drug is bound to plasma proteins by 94%, and less than 0.5% is excreted unchanged in urine. Hepatic elimination is rapid: t1/2 beta is 2.4 +/- 0.8 hr (mean +/- S.D) and total body clearance is 283 +/- 43 ml/min (plasma) or 502 +/- 105 ml/min (blood). This substantial first-pass effect leads to bioavailability of only 44%, despite very rapid absorption (t1/2abs = 0.23 +/- 0.37 hr) after oral dosing. There is good intraindividual linear correlations (r between 0.68 and 0.97) between plasma levels and dynamic effects, as assessed by the d2 letter cancellation test and a sedation index formed from visual analogue scales.

descriptions.text: Our subjects were six healthy males (ages 25 to 37 yr; five nonsmokers, one smoker)
Normal food was first allowed at lunch time.
After fasting overnight, the subjects received a single oral dose of 15 mg midazolam.
Two of the subjects also received a dose of 7.5 mg.
All also received midazolam (0.075 mg/kg) intravenously over a 1-min period.

Four subjects underwent a consecutive bolus/infusion experiment with dosage increments every 2 hr. The corresponding doses were calculated according to kinetic parameters obtained from the previous intravenous study to approach effective plasma levels (see Table II).

reference.name: Backman1998
reference.title: The area under the plasma concentration-time curve for oral midazolam is 400-fold larger during treatment with itraconazole than with rifampicin.
reference.abstract: Switching from inhibition to induction of cytochrome P450 3A (CYP3A) enzymes causes a very great (400-fold) change in the AUC of oral midazolam. During oral administration of CYP3A substrates that undergo extensive first-pass metabolism, similar changes in pharmacokinetics are expected to occur when potent inhibitors or inducers of CYP3A are added to the treatment. After cessation of treatment with itraconazole or rifampicin, the risk of significant interaction continues up to at least 4 days, probably even longer.

descriptions.text: Nine volunteers (5 women and 4 men, age range 22–34 years, weight range 55–78 kg) participated
The subjects were ascertained to be healthy.
Apart from the use of contraceptive steroids (two subjects), none of the subjects was receiving continuous medication.
Five of the subjects were smokers.
They were not allowed to smoke tobacco or ingest coffee, tea, alcohol, efruit, or efruit juice during the study.
All the subjects participated in five study phases. During each phase, the subjects ingested a single dose of either 7.5 mg or 15 mg midazolam with 150 ml water at 1300 hours on one test day
Phase I: During phase I (control, no pretreatment), a 15-mg dose of midazolam was ingested.
Phase II: 1 week later subjects started to ingest 200 mg itraconazole daily at 1100 hours for 4 days. On day 4, each subject ingested a 7.5-mg dose of midazolam.
Phase III: Four days after this, the subjects were given another 7.5-mg dose of midazolam.

Phase IV/V: Two weeks later, they started to ingest rifampicin 600 mg once daily at 2000 hours for 5 days. On the first (phase IV) and fourth (phase V) day after the last dose of rifampicin, the subjects ingested 15 mg midazolam.

reference.name: Bornemann1985
reference.title: Dose dependent pharmacokinetics of midazolam.
reference.abstract: The pharmacokinetics of midazolam and 1-hydroxymethylmidazolam were investigated following oral administration of 7.5, 15 and 30 mg doses of midazolam in solution to 12 healthy subjects. Compared to the 7.5 mg dose, the Cmax and AUC parameters of both midazolam and 1-hydroxymethylmidazolam increased proportionally after the 15 mg dose and more than proportionally after the 30 mg dose. The t1/2 for midazolam remained relatively constant between the 7.5 and 15 mg doses whereas it increased slightly but significantly after the 30 mg dose. These data indicated that the pharmacokinetics of midazolam and 1-hydroxymethylmidazolam were linear between the 7.5 and 15 mg oral dose range. However, after the 30 mg dose, the systemic availability of midazolam and the AUC for 1-hydroxymethylmidazolam appeared to be greater than that anticipated from the lower doses, possibly due to saturation of midazolam first-pass metabolism. This is not expected to have any clinical significance under the conditions of therapeutic use.

descriptions.text: Twelve healthy volunteers (8 males and 4 females) ranging in age from 24 to 52 years (mean age, 38 years) and in body weight from 54.9 to 92.5 kg (mean weight, 73.0 kg) were impaneled. The volunteers were in good general health.
Volunteers received a 7.5, 15 or 30 mg dose of midazolam in solution followed by 120 ml of water in a three-way randomized crossover design with a oneweek washout after each dose.

reference.name: Chaobal2005
reference.title: Single-point sampling for assessment of constitutive, induced, and inhibited cytochrome P450 3A activity with alfentanil or midazolam.
reference.abstract: A single plasma concentration can predict alfentanil AUC and hence clearance, independent of dose, and thus appears to be an effective and efficient strategy for assessing CYP3A modulation over a broad spectrum of enzyme activity.

descriptions.text: 10 healthy volunteers participated in this study.

Volunteers on each of 5 occasions received oral midazolam (3 mg) followed 1 hour later by oral alfentanil and on 4 occasions received intravenous midazolam (1 mg) followed 1 hour later by intravenous alfentanil
Control: no pretreatment; alfentanil, 15 ug/kg intravenously and 23 and 60 ug/kg orally)
Hepatic and intestinal CYP3A induction: Rifampicin for 5 days; alfentanil, 15 ug/kg intravenously and 60 ug/kg orally
Hepatic and intestinal CYP3A inhibition: Troleandomycin 2 hours before midazolam; alfentanil, 15 ug/kg intravenously and 23 ug/kg orally
Selective intestinal CYP3A inhibition: Grapefruit juice; alfentanil, 15 ug/kg intravenously and 23 ug/kg orally

comments.text: The study doesn't mention the administered doses of rifampicin, troleandomycin and grapefruit juices, administration time(s) of grapefruit juice, and form of oral midazolam. All these details were taken from the referred study 'Kharasch2004' (pmid: 15536460)

reference.name: Dyk2019
reference.title: Validation of a 3-h Sampling Interval to Assess Variability in Cytochrome P450 3A Phenotype and the Impact of Induction and Mechanism-Based Inhibition Using Midazolam as a Probe Substrate.

descriptions.text: 30 healthy males participated in this study.

Subjects (n = 30) recieved 1 mg oral midazolam at baseline (Day 1), following a 7 day course of rifampin (Induction phase; day 1-8) with 1 mg oral midazolam on day 8, and following a 3 day course of immediate release clarithromycin (inhibition phase; day 12-14) with 1 mg oral midazolam on day 15.

reference.name: Eap2004
reference.title: Oral administration of a low dose of midazolam (75 microg) as an in vivo probe for CYP3A activity.
reference.abstract: A low oral dose of midazolam can be used to phenotype CYP3A, either by the determination of total 1'OH-midazolam/midazolam ratios at 30 min or by the determination of midazolam plasma levels between 1.5 h and 4 h after its administration.

descriptions.text: 21 healthy volunteers participated (19 Caucasian, 1 North African, 1 black African; 8 male; mean±SD, range: age 33±9 years, 23–55 years; weight 62±10 kg, 50–78 kg)
Subjects were all free of drugs with the exception of a subject with an oral contraceptive.
Subjects were not allowed to drink grapefruit juice 1 week prior to the study, and no alcohol and caffeine-containing beverages during the study sessions
Control group recieved orally 7.5 mg midazolam in one session and 75 ug in a second session.

In a second group subjects recieved 75 ug oral midazolam in the first session. 2 days before the second session they recieved 200 mg ketoconazole twice daily for two days and then again 75 ug oral midazolam.

A third group recived 75 ug oral midazolam in the first session. 4 days before the second session they recieved 450 mg rifampicin daily for 4 days and on the 5th day 75 ug midazolam. After the last blood sampling of the second session they recieved one more dose of rifampicin and 7.5 mg oral midazolam the following day.

reference.name: Eeckhoudt2001
reference.title: Midazolam and cortisol metabolism before and after CYP3A induction in humans.
reference.abstract: These data demonstrate that there is a strong positive correlation between systemic midazolam clearance and 6beta-OH cortisol urinary ratio before and after induction. This suggests that the 6beta-OH cortisol urinary ratio test is a non-invasive alternative to the use of systemic midazolam clearance for monitoring the time-course of CYP3A induction.
reference.journal: International journal of clinical pharmacology and therapeutics

descriptions.text: Eight healthy white volunteers (age: 23 – 53 years; body weight: 46 – 91 kg), 6 men and 2 women, participated in the study.
One was a smoker. All subjects consumed alcohol infrequently (< 40 g/week) or not at all.
Subjects took no medication during the week preceding the study period and during the study.
Midazolam (40 ug/kg, i.v. bolus) was administered on 3 different occasions: Day 0, Day 7 and Day 10. On Days 1 – 6, the volunteers ingested 600 mg of rifampin per day

reference.name: Erp2011
reference.title: Marginal increase of sunitinib exposure by grapefruit juice.
reference.abstract: GJ consumption results in a marginal increase in sunitinib exposure which is not considered clinically relevant. There is no clinical evidence underscoring the warning in the sunitinib drug label regarding concomitant use of GJ.

descriptions.text: 8 cancer patients (1 male, 7 female) with a median age of 54 years (41 years - 78 years) participated in this study.

The patient should have a tumor for which sunitinib is registered as the first-line (metastatic renal cell carcinoma), second-line (gastrointestinal stromal tumor) treatment or for which no treatment options were available anymore.
The study was performed during one sunitinib treatment cycle of 6 weeks. The first PK day was at steady-state sunitinib PK (between day 14–20) and the second PK day was on day 28.
On days 25, 26 and 27, the patients used 200-ml grapefruit juice 3 times daily. On these 3 days, sunitinib was simultaneously used with the morning consumption of the grapefruit juice.
On both PK days, patients were given one midazolam 7.5-mg tablet

reference.name: Farkas2007
reference.title: Pomegranate juice does not impair clearance of oral or intravenous midazolam, a probe for cytochrome P450-3A activity: comparison with grapefruit juice.
reference.abstract: The effect of pomegranate juice (PJ) or grapefruit juice (GFJ) on CYP3A activity was studied in vitro and in healthy human volunteers. In human liver microsomes, the mean 50% inhibitory concentrations (IC(50)) for PJ and GFJ versus CYP3A (triazolam alpha-hydroxylation) were 0.61% and 0.55%, (v/v) respectively, without preincubation of inhibitor with microsomes. After preincubation, the IC(50) for PJ increased to 0.97% (P < .05), whereas the IC(50) for GFJ decreased to 0.41% (P < .05), suggesting mechanism-based inhibition by GFJ but not PJ. Pretreatment of volunteer subjects (n = 13) with PJ (8 oz) did not alter the elimination half-life, volume of distribution, or clearance of intravenous midazolam (2 mg). Administration of PJ also did not affect C(max), total area under the curve (AUC), or clearance of oral midazolam (6 mg). However, GFJ (8 oz) increased midazolam C(max) and AUC by a factor of 1.3 and 1.5, respectively, and reduced oral clearance to 72% of control values. Thus, PJ does not alter clearance of intravenous or oral midazolam, whereas GFJ impairs clearance and elevates plasma levels of oral midazolam.

descriptions.text: The final data analysis is based on 13 subjects who completed all 5 trials.
They were nonsmokers, free of medical disease, and taking no medications.
mean age, 35 ± 8 years; mean weight, 81 ± 10 kg. The racial/ethnic distribution was: 9 Caucasian, 2 Black, 1 Hispanic, and 1 Asian.

comments.text: The study started with 15 subjects. However, due to dropouts, the data analysis is based on only 13 subjects

descriptions.text: There were five interventions:
1. Intravenous (IV) midazolam (2 mg) preceded by water
2. Oral midazolam (6 mg) preceded by water
3. IV midazolam (2 mg) preceded by pomegranate juice
4. Oral midazolam (6 mg) preceded by pomegranate juice
5. Oral midazolam (6 mg) preceded by grapefruit juice
Subjects recieved fruit beverages resp. water the evening before and 1 hour bevore midazolam admission

reference.name: Gorski2003
reference.title: The effect of age, sex, and rifampin administration on intestinal and hepatic cytochrome P450 3A activity.
reference.abstract: Sex-related differences exist in the extent of intestinal and hepatic CYP3A induction by rifampin. The extent of induction at hepatic and intestinal sites was inversely dependent and reflected the independent regulation of CYP3A expression at these sites. The large interindividual variation in the extent of induction is explained in part by the variation in baseline expression of CYP3A. Sex-related differences in response to CYP3A inducers will be substrate-dependent and reflect the relative contribution of hepatic and intestinal sites of metabolism.

descriptions.text:
Fourteen young women (mean age, 26 ± 4 years), 14 young men (mean age, 27 ± 4 years), 14
elderly women (mean age, 72 ± 5 years), and 10 elderly men (mean age, 70 ± 4 years) received simultaneous
intravenous doses (0.05 mg/kg over a 30-minute period) and oral doses of midazolam (3-8 mg of a stable
isotope, 15N3-midazolam) before and after 7 days of rifampin dosing (600 mg once daily in the evening).

14 young men (mean ± SD age, 27 ± 4 years), 14 young women (mean ± SD age, 26 ± 4 years), 10 elderly men (mean ± SD age, 70 ± 4 years), and 14 elderly women (mean +-  D age, 72 ± 5 years) participated in the study
All volunteers were nonsmokers.
All young male and female individuals were without significant medical conditions as assessed by medical history, physical examination, and blood and urine chemistry screening tests.
None of the female volunteers was pregnant or breast-feeding.
Some elderly volunteers had chronic stable diseases as shown in Table I.

Volunteers were prohibited from taking nonstudy medications, except for the elderly patients, who were allowed to receive stable doses of medications that were not inducers, inhibitors, or substrates for CYP3A4.
Information about subjects in Table 2.
All participants abstained from ethanol- and grapefruit juice–containing foods 1 week before the start of the study and throughout the study.
The volunteers simultaneously received a 4-mg dose of 15N3-midazolam orally in solution form and a 0.05-mg/kg dose of midazolam intravenously over a 30-minute period.
After the 24-hour blood withdrawal, volunteers received rifampin, 600 mg orally, once daily, in the evening for 7 days.
On the morning of the seventh day of dosing, the participants received a 6-mg dose of 15N3-midazolam orally and a 0.05-mg/kg midazolam infusion intravenously over a 30-minute period.

reference.name: Greenblatt1986
reference.title: Absence of interaction of cimetidine and ranitidine with intravenous and oral midazolam.
reference.abstract: Eight healthy volunteers received a single 5-mg intravenous dose of the imidazobenzodiazepine derivative midazolam on three occasions in random sequence: a, control, with no other treatment; b, during coadministration of cimetidine, 300 mg every 6 hr; c, during coadministration of ranitidine, 150 mg every 12 hr. Midazolam kinetics in each trial were determined from multiple plasma midazolam levels measured by gas chromatography for 24 hr after each dose. High pressure liquid chromatography analysis of plasma also verified compliance with cimetidine (mean level, 0.61 microgram/ml) and ranitidine (mean level, 0.36 microgram/ml) regimens. Analysis of variance indicated no significant differences in mean values for trials a, b, and c in midazolam elimination half-life (2.25 vs 2.02 vs 2.05 hr), volume of distribution (2.13 vs 2.14 vs 2.16 L/kg) or total clearance (10.8 vs 12.2 vs 12.3 ml . min-1 . kg-1). In a second study, six subjects received a 15-mg oral dose of midazolam on three occasions identical to those described above. Again, there were no significant differences among trials a, b, and c in midazolam peak plasma level (90 vs 95 vs 117 ng/ml), time of peak level (0.65 vs 1.45 vs 0.90 hr after dose), elimination half-life (3.04 vs 3.38 vs 3.30 hr), or apparent oral clearance (16.2 vs 14.3 vs 13.8 ml . min-1 . kg-1). Thus the usual therapeutic doses of cimetidine or ranitidine do not significantly alter the kinetics of intravenous or oral midazolam in healthy individuals.

descriptions.text: Intravenous Midazolam Study: Eight healthy volunteers, aged 22-46 yr, participated. Five were male and three were female. All were healthy, active, ambulatory adults with no evidence of medical disease and receiving no other medications.

Oral Midazolam Study: Six healthy male volunteers (different from the subjects who took part in the intravenous midazolam study) participated. All were healthy, active, ambulatory adults with no evidence of medical disease and receiving no other medications.
On each occasion they received a single 5-mg intravenous dose of midazolam by 1-min intravenous infusion.

Midazolam administered alone, without concurrent medications (control); midazolam given intravenously during coadministration of cimetidine, 300 mg 4 times daily, beginning 24 hr before and continuing for 24 hr after midazolam administration; and midazolam given intravenously during coadministration of ranitidine, 150 mg every 12 hr,beginning 24 hr before and continuing for 24 hr aftermidazolam administration.

reference.name: Greenblatt2004
reference.title: Kinetics and EEG effects of midazolam during and after 1-minute, 1-hour, and 3-hour intravenous infusions.
reference.abstract: The objective of this study was to evaluate the kinetics and dynamics of midazolam when administered by three different infusion schemes, using electroencephalography to measure pharmacodynamic effects. In a three-way crossover study, 8 volunteers received midazolam (0.1 mg/kg) by constant-rate intravenous infusion. The durations of midazolam infusions for the three trials were 1 minute, 1 hour, and 3 hours. Plasma midazolam concentrations and electroencephalographic (EEG) activity in the 13- to 30-Hz range were monitored for 24 hours. Based on separate analysis of each subject-trial, mean values for volume of distribution and distribution or elimination half-life did not significantly vary. Central compartment volume and clearance differed among the three midazolam infusion trials; however, the magnitude of change was small. EEG activity in the 13- to 30-Hz range significantly increased for all three midazolam infusion trials. Plots of midazolam plasma concentration versus pharmacodynamic EEG effect for the 1-hour and 3-hour infusion trials did not reveal evidence of either counterclockwise or clockwise hysteresis. Plots from the 1-minute infusion trial demonstrated counterclockwise hysteresis, consistent with an equilibration effect-site delay. This was incorporated into a kinetic-dynamic model in which hypothetical effect-site concentration was related to pharmacodynamic EEG effect via the sigmoid E(max) model. Analysis of all three infusion trials together yielded the following mean estimates: maximum EEG effect, 16.3% over baseline; 50% maximum effective concentration, 31 ng/mL; and an apparent rate constant for drug disappearance from the effect compartment which approached infinity. Despite the delay in effect onset during the 1-minute midazolam infusion, midazolam infusions in duration of up to 3 hours produce CNS sedation without evidence of tolerance.

descriptions.text: Eight healthy male volunteers, ages 32 to 44 years, participated.
All subjects were healthy and recieved no medication. Six of the 8 subjects were cigarette smokers.
Subjects participated in a three-way crossover study, with at least 1 week elapsing between trials.
On each trial, a 0.1-mg/kg dose of midazolam was given intravenously by constant-rate infusion. The infusion durations for the three trials were 1 minute, 1 hour, and 3 hours.

reference.name: Kawaguchi-Suzuki2017
reference.title: Effect of Low-Furanocoumarin Hybrid Grapefruit Juice Consumption on Midazolam Pharmacokinetics.
reference.abstract: The objectives of this study were to investigate the effect of grapefruit juice low in furanocoumarins on CYP3A activity and to summarize previous findings of enzyme inhibition measured by the metabolism of midazolam after intake of grapefruit juice. Twelve healthy volunteers participated in a prospective, randomized, double-blinded, 3-way crossover clinical study to determine the effect of regular grapefruit juice (RGJ) and a novel, low-furanocoumarin hybrid grapefruit juice (HGJ) on the metabolism of oral midazolam, used as a probe for in vivo CYP3A activity, compared with water as a control. The RGJ was 100% hand-squeezed "Hudson" grapefruit juice, and the HGJ contained low amounts of furanocoumarin constituents. The point estimates (90% confidence intervals) for the RGJ/water midazolam AUC geometric mean ratio was 122% (107-140). The point estimate for the HGJ/water midazolam AUC ratio was within the 80% to 125% bioequivalence range, indicating an absence of interaction. This finding also prompted a systematic review of available evidence on the pharmacokinetic alteration of midazolam by grapefruit juice. Although most studies demonstrated alteration in midazolam pharmacokinetics supporting inhibition of CYP3A activity as a likely mechanism, the cohorts included in these studies and the extent of the pharmacokinetic interaction varied widely. The current study indicated grapefruit juice-drug interaction varies substantially based on patient characteristics and/or grapefruit juice product-related factors, including the amount of furanocoumarin constituents present in the juice.

descriptions.text: Twelve healthy participants (aged 18-55 years, 30.8 ± 11.8 years, mean ± standard deviation) completed all study sessions.
8 were female and 4 were male.
The body mass index (BMI) was 24.7 ± 4.9, and 2 participants were considered obese (BMI >= 30).
The participants of this study were ethnically diverse (10 whites [2 Hispanics, 8 non-Hispanics], 1 African American, and 1 Asian).
Exclusion criteria included age <18 years old, pregnancy or breast-feeding, current smoking, and diagnosis of a chronic condition or use of chronic medications except oral contraceptives.

All participants abstained from consuming citrus fruit products for 1 week, alcoholic beverages for 24 hours, herbal containing supplements/teas/beverages and over-thecounter medications for 48 hours prior to each study session.
Subjects recieved 200 mL of either water, “Hudson” hand-squeezed 100% regular efruit juice (RGJ), or hybrid grapefruit juice (HGJ) daily for 3 consecutive days, administered in the morning.
On the third day a 5-mg dose of oral midazolam syrup was administered immediately following the third dose of randomized treatment.

reference.name: Kharasch2004
reference.title: Intravenous and oral alfentanil as in vivo probes for hepatic and first-pass cytochrome P450 3A activity: noninvasive assessment by use of pupillary miosis.
reference.abstract: ALF and MDZ have similar intestinal extraction but low and intermediate hepatic extraction, respectively. Systemic and oral clearances of ALF are excellent in vivo probes for hepatic and first-pass CYP3A activities and drug interactions. Miosis was an acceptable surrogate for plasma ALF. ALF miosis may be a suitable noninvasive in vivo probe for both hepatic and first-pass CYP3A.

descriptions.text: 10 volunteers (5 men and 5 women) participated in this investigation (mean age, 27 +/- 6 years [range, 20-39 years]); (mean weight, 74 +/- 16 kg [range, 53-96 kg])

Subjects  were in good health with no remarkable medical problems, were within 20% of ideal body weight, had no history of hepatic or renal disease, and were taking no prescription or nonprescription medications known to alter CYP3A activity, including oral contraceptives
Both smokers and nonsmokers were enrolled.
Subjects were instructed to consume no grapefruit-containing foods or juices for 5 days before each study session and on the study day and no alcohol or caffeine for 24 hours before each study

Control: 1-mg IV bolus of MDZ, followed 1 hour later by a 15-ug/kg IV bolus of ALF; 3 mg oral MDZ, followed 1 hour later by 23 ug/kg oral ALF; and 3 mg oral MDZ, followed 1 hour later by 60 ug/kg oral ALF

Induction: Rifampin (INN, rifampicin) (600 mg) was administered orally at bedtime for 5 days; 1 mg IV MDZ was then administered, followed 1 hour later by administration of 15 ug/kg IV ALF, rifampin at bedtime, and on the next day 3 mg oral MDZ, followed 1 hour later by 60 ug/kg oral ALF.

hepatic and intestinal CYP3A Inhibition: Troleandomycin (TAO) (500 mg) was administered orally approximately 2 hours before MDZ (1 mg intravenously, followed 1 hour later by 15 ug/kg ALF intravenously) and again 6 hours later; on the next day TAO (500 mg) was administered orally approximately 2 hours before MDZ (3 mg orally, followed 1 hour later by 23 ug/kg oral ALF) and again 6 hours later

Selective intestinal CYP3A inhibition: Grapefruit juice (8 oz) was given orally at bedtime; double-strength grapefruit juice (3 oz) was then given the following morning, followed by 1 mg IV MDZ and followed 1 hour later by 15 ug/kg IV ALF; grapefruit juice (8 oz) was given orally at bedtime, and 3 mg MDZ was then given orally with double-strength grapefruit juice (3 oz) the following morning, followed 1 hour later by 23 ug/kg oral ALF

reference.name: Kupferschmidt1995
reference.title: Interaction between grapefruit juice and midazolam in humans.
reference.abstract: Pretreatment with grapefruit juice is associated with increased bioavailability and changes in the pharmacodynamics of midazolam that may be clinically important, particularly in patients with other causes for increased midazolam bioavailability such as advanced age, cirrhosis of the liver, and administration of other inhibitors of cytochrome P450.

descriptions.text: Eight healthy male volunteers with a mean +/- SEM age of 25 */- 1 years and a mean body weight of 70 +/- 1 kg.
The subjects fasted for 10 hours before the administration of midazolam.
They also had to avoid caffeine, nicotine, alcohol, and citrus fruit products for 1 week before and throughout the study period.
The subjects received randomly either 200 ml water or 200 ml of normal strength grapefruit juice 60 and 15 minutes before the oral or intravenous administration of 15 or 5 mg midazolam
Intravenous (5 mg) or oral (15 mg) midazolam was administered after pretreatment with water or grapefruit juice.
A light standard meal was served 7 hours after the administration of midazolam.
The interval between the individual sessions was at least 2 weeks.
